Title: Structural Insights into 4,5-DOPA Extradiol Dioxygenase from Beta vulgaris: Unraveling the Key Step in Versatile Betalain Biosynthesis
Journal: Journal of Agricultural and Food Chemistry

Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Central chromophoric intermediate for betalain pigment biosynthesis; betalains are widely used in food, beverage, and medicinal industries
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Catalyzes conversion of L-DOPA to betalamic acid, the key intermediate in betalain biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: directly forms key intermediate leading to betalain end products
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Supported as a key step in betalain biosynthesis; rate-limiting not directly established
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Catalyzes extradiol ring cleavage of L-DOPA to generate 4,5-seco-DOPA, which spontaneously cyclizes to betalamic acid
Pathway_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: Class III extradiol dioxygenase family; nonheme iron-dependent extradiol dioxygenase
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Heterologously expressed in E. coli BL21 (DE3)
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: In vitro assay conditions; optimal activity at pH 8.5 and 50 °C; Fe2+ required as essential cofactor; ascorbic acid included in assay
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Recombinant expression and purification; in vitro enzyme assay; HPLC and MS product identification; steady-state kinetics; metal analysis by ICP-MS and X-ray absorption spectroscopy; crystal structure determination; molecular docking; site-directed mutagenesis
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. BvDOD from Beta vulgaris was structurally characterized as a monomeric nonheme Fe-dependent extradiol dioxygenase. 2. Recombinant BvDOD converted L-DOPA into betalamic acid confirmed by HPLC and MS. 3. The enzyme showed highest activity at pH 8.5 and 50 °C and depended strongly on Fe2+. 4. Structural analysis identified His15, His53, and His229 as Fe-ligand residues in the active site. 5. Docking and mutagenesis demonstrated that His119 and His175 are essential for catalysis, while Thr17, Asp254, and Tyr260 are crucial for substrate positioning and catalytic efficiency.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful target for metabolic engineering of betalain production; structural knowledge and catalytic residues can guide enzyme optimization for improved betalain synthesis in biotechnology or crop engineering
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Study focuses on biochemical and structural characterization in heterologous bacterial expression rather than plant transgenic validation; several mutated residues (H119A, H175A, T17A, D254A, Y260A) caused near-complete loss of activity but are residue variants rather than separate core genes
